tagged_story_for_tts:
False facts. Confident mistakes.

The term AI hallucination borrows from human psychology—when a mind perceives things that aren’t there. But here, it’s code inventing answers: a chatbot insisting the Eiffel Tower is in Rome, or a medical AI diagnosing a disease that doesn’t exist.

It happens because AIs don’t know—they predict. Trained on billions of words, they guess the next likely phrase, like autocomplete on steroids. Ask for "the capital of France," and it replies "Paris" not from memory, but because that’s the statistically safest bet. But ask for "the capital of Wakanda," and it might fabricate "Birnin Zana," complete with fake history. No alarm bells ring; confidence is built into the design.

Real-world stakes: A lawyer cited fictitious cases in court. A self-driving car misread a stop sign as a speed limit. The risk isn’t malice—it’s overtrust. Tools like retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) force AIs to cite sources, but even those can be misread or outdated.

Three related subjects are ai training data flaws, black box problem details, and human bias in algorithms.
